data.title: Coles: A Steady Grocery Player In Post-Hike Australia, Though Fully Priced
data.description:
In the highly concentrated grocery retail market "Down Under", the Hawthorn East, Australia-based chain Coles Group (OTCPK:CLEGF) holds the second-largest market share in the country (28%), just behind Woolworths (OTCPK:WOLWF) with 37%, which practically controls 65% of the entire market in Australia.

The characteristic of Coles' business, as it is part of the consumer staples segment, is, in theory, non-cyclical, with inelastic demand and generally resilient in periods of economic downturn. For this reason, much of the investment thesis is based on resilience and shareholder returns, and Coles has distributed attractive dividends.

Although over the last two years, with inflation peaking in the post-pandemic scenario and Australia's central bank raising rates to historic levels, Coles' business has not been so immune, and its share price performance has fluctuated in line with the broader market. Likewise, the company's margins and comparable sales followed similar patterns.

But in any case, Coles continues to generate great value for its shareholders, above its cost of capital, showing robust fundamentals within a sector characterized by high-volume and tight margins.

Of course, this comes at a cost. Coles' shares are not particularly cheap, and trade above its main peers. Although the premium is not unfounded, I believe that valuations may be indicating at least a well-priced business, but one that is worth monitoring for any corrections, and from the perspective of including it in a dividend portfolio.

(Note: Coles trades on the OTC markets as an unsponsored ADR. Therefore, its liquidity and information are limited. For more information, I recommend following the ticker on the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX) by the ticker “COL”.)

A Quick Overview Of Coles And The Australian Retail Grocery Market Landscape

If you're in Australia and need to go grocery shopping, you have the following options: Coles—being the chain with the widest diversification of products and brands, and competitive, moderate prices; Woolworths (or Woolies)—the largest chain by market share, the widest variety of products (and potentially the best in-store experience) but with prices that are often more expensive than Coles; then Aldi—the German chain which sells mostly own brands at a more affordable price; Metcash (IGA) (OTCPK:MCSHF), more focused on smaller-format stores but with prices generally higher than those of large chains; and Costco (COST) which has around 15 warehouse locations in major cities.

Above all, this shows that the supermarket sector in Australia is highly concentrated and has high barriers to entry.

Specifically on the Coles side, supermarkets are obviously its core business, but it also operates on the liquor front, under the Liquorland brand, and also operates convenience stores in petrol stations (Express), although it has divested its fuel business.

The performance of Coles' shares on the ASX (Australian stock exchange), in line with Australian equities, represented by the iShares MSCI Australia ETF (EWA) has engaged in a strong rally since the beginning of last year, driven largely by reasons that I attribute to the prospects of a reduced inflation scenario (currently at 2.4% and within the central bank's target) and falling cash rates (at 3.85% from 4.1% early this year).

Coles: competitive positioning metrics

More specifically, with regard to the Coles business, I believe that it is first crucial to analyze its operational quality and competitive resilience. And the first step is to track Coles' top line and bottom-line performance over the last five years.

As the graph above shows, Coles' Group (Supermarket+Liquor) increased steadily from ~AUD 37B in 2020 to over AUD 44B in 2024, despite a slight dip in 2022 as the pandemic boom eased with the economic reopening, but managed to rebound strongly in 2023 and 2024.

At the same time, Coles Group's operating margins peaked in 2021 during the pandemic at ~4.85%, had a natural decline in 2022, but only in 2023, at the height of monetary tightening, bottomed out at ~4.55% until recovering again in 2024 to ~4.7%. These swings may seem irrelevant at first glance, but in a sector that usually has razor-thin margins, an increase of 0.15 or 0.2 percentage points represents millions of AUD in additional profits, given the huge scale of revenues.

It also shows that, despite Coles' theoretically being an anti-cyclical company in the face of macro headwinds, the swing in operating margins coincided with the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) starting an aggressive cash rate hike cycle, where cash rates went from 0.1% before the pandemic to as high as 4.35% in 2023. And this has resulted in strong pressure from grocery chains like Coles not to pass on 100% of inflation to the consumer, which squeezes margins.

So, as we can see, the recovery in margins in 2024 clearly showed a respite for Coles as inflation began to slow and interest rate rises stabilized. The 2024 recovery indicates better cost control, productivity improvements, or improved sales mix.

The gains in margins and efficiency last year are also attributable to the investments Coles has continued to make over the last five years. The company invested around AU$6 billion in CapEx in order to modernize its supply chain, distribution, and fulfillment centers. As a result, Coles has managed to achieve fulfillment rates of 99% and has also managed to increase its product range by a quarter compared to a typical Coles store.

The other metric I'd like to focus on is Coles' same-store sales growth. As the graph below shows, supermarket comps sales fell from ~6% in 2020 during the pandemic to as low as 2.5% between 2021-2022, but returned to 5.5% in 2023 mainly with product availability and customer retention initiatives (also attributable to store investments), despite a brief correction in 2024, but still at rates below the pandemic period.

The most negative side has been the comp sales in liquor. There was a decline from ~7% in 2020 to nearly -1.5% in 2024. This performance, I think, is more serious because it is a reflection of not only increased competition, but also changing consumer behavior and regulatory taxes harming the liquor industry in Australia. Therefore, I attribute structural headwinds to the underperformance from 2022 onwards.

Now, bringing it back to more recent results, in 3Q25, reported in April this year, Coles saw supermarket comp sales growing at 3%, down from 4.7% in the same period last year, but with an acceleration in e-commerce sales jumping 25.7% from 3Q24, already making up more than 11% of the company's total revenues.

On the liquidity side, a brief recovery in comp sales, falling just 2% versus 3.1% in the same period last year, can also be attributed to greater penetration of digital channels.

According to the management team, expectations for the 4Q25 results are as follows:

“In the early part of the fourth quarter, Supermarkets sales growth has remained broadly in line with the third quarter with continued volume growth supported by a positive response from customers across our Easter offer. In Liquor, we have continued to see positive sales growth underpinned by the success of our recent value campaigns.”

A Brief Look At Coles’ Financial Health

For a capital-intensive, low-margin business like Coles, solvency, capital efficiency, and cash generation are key to having a broader vision of how the business has returned value to its shareholders.

In this case, Coles has been able to efficiently transform its invested capital into profits, as it has had an expressive Return on Capital (ROC)—calculated from EBIT/Capital Employed (total assets minus current liabilities). Over the last five years, Coles has had an average ROC of 16.3%, increasing by 1% from 2021 to 2025, to recently reach 16.6%. Considering that Coles has an estimated cost of equity of ~6%, given a low beta and risk-free rates of 3.85%, the ROC is well above its cost of capital, indicating consistent value creation for its shareholders.

The second point is Coles' liquidity and leverage. Currently, the supermarket chain has a conservative capital structure with a balance sheet leverage ratio of 2.7x, less leveraged than five years ago when it was 3.1x, but at the same time slightly below the 2.6x in 2023.

The final, yet equally important, metric I bring up is cash flows. Coles' cash realization ratio has exceeded 100% in the last five years, with the exception of last year, in 2024, when it stood at 98%. In this case, that implies a drop following the extent to which Coles had been able to convert its earnings into actual cash. In this case, the drop was attributable to rising working capital, the result of inventory build-up.

But in general, the AU$2.8 billion/year in operating cash reiterates the slack in remunerating shareholders without having to take on debt or sell assets, which makes Coles a good dividend payer. Over the last five years, Coles' payout has ranged from 81% of its profits, increasing dividends over the last six years, and currently paying a yield of 3.17%—just below the risk-free rate in Australia.

Risks And Final Considerations

The retail grocery landscape in Australia is highly competitive, and price wars tend to be the primary risk of the thesis, leading to potential market share erosion and margin pressures. Furthermore, the ongoing trade wars tend to spill over into the sector as a whole, and Coles, being no exception, might not be able to pass all cost increases onto customers while keeping operating profits intact.

However, taking into account that in the last twelve months, Coles' operating profits totaled AU$1.98 billion while the company trades at an enterprise value of AU$39.5 billion, this equated to an earnings yield (EBIT/EV) of 5%, being, in theory, lower than its cost of capital and suggesting a stock at least fairly priced.

Nor does Coles' ADR trade below its main domestic peers Woolworths and Metcash, although it pays a dividend yield about the same as its main competitor—Woolworths—and boasts traditional operating metrics of lower ROIC/ROCE at 9%/14.4% vs. 13%/21%, respectively.

That said, for valuation reasons, I see Coles' ADR as a Hold at present, even though the company should continue to return value to its shareholders above its cost of capital.

data.title: An Innovative Approach To Benchmarking Canadian Credit Risk
data.description:
By Joshua Gorelik, Senior Manager, Quantitative Product Research, Fixed Income, Currencies and Commodities | Marina Mets, Head of Fixed Income, Currencies and Commodities, Americas

Introduction

FTSE Russell is introducing an innovative index solution that is designed to capture the dynamics of Canadian corporate credit risk. Through extensive research and in-depth analysis, we have constructed a liquid, simple and transparent benchmark that isolates the credit spread component in Canadian bank bonds to provide a standardised gauge of credit market conditions in Canada.

The FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series captures the yield differential (spread) between a basket of domestic Canadian bank bonds and benchmark Government of Canada securities, serving as a measure of Canadian financial sector credit risk.

The indices within the series are transparent and designed to be replicable, with individual security holdings, prices and spreads disclosed daily. In this way, the index series addresses the need for representative spread products focused on the Canadian fixed income market.

In this paper, we show how the design of the index series balances capacity, tradeability and the accuracy of spread measurement. We explain the indices’ construction methodology and look at simulated past performance, showing that the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index can act as a hedge for a broad range of corporate bond exposures.

Functions of bank credit spreads

Corporate bonds’ credit spreads are often used as leading indicators for economic activity because of the relationship between the quality of borrowers' balance sheets and their access to external finance[1].

For example, credit spreads started to rise sharply from summer 2007 onwards[2], over a year before the default of Lehman Brothers, serving as an advance warning of deteriorating financial market conditions.

Within the corporate bond sector, banks’ credit spreads merit particular attention. Given the high repeat issuance levels and relatively higher liquidity of bank-issued corporate bonds, market shocks or periods of credit spread compression are usually captured more quickly in banks than in the broader corporate bond universe.

This is especially true of Canada, where banks represent a large proportion of the overall corporate sector of the FTSE Canada Universe Bond Index[3], a flagship measure of the Canadian bond market.

An index that quickly reflects market perceptions of bank credit risk therefore serves as a valuable tool not just for corporate bond investors, but for the broader financial markets. Such an index can serve as a benchmark for broader credit market conditions, a key risk measure in asset allocation models and as a pricing tool in other financial contracts (such as listed derivatives).

Exhibit 1: Functions of bank credit spreads

Design principles for a bank credit spread index

FTSE Russell is committed to fostering evolution in the markets we serve. We have been working closely with key industry partners to support the development of credit risk transfer solutions for the Canadian market. Through extensive research and in-depth analysis, we have designed a liquid, simple and transparent benchmark that isolates the credit spread component in bank-issued corporate debt to provide a standardised gauge of credit market conditions in Canada.

To ensure broad adoption and ease of use across a variety of market applications, FTSE’s approach to constructing the FTSE Canada Credit Spread Index series was guided by several considerations:

Liquidity: the basket of index securities should be highly liquid;

Simplicity: the index’s construction methodology and weight allocation should be easy to understand; and

Transparency: the index’s selection methodology should be replicable by index users and the index data should be available for access.

Building the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series

The construction of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series involves three stages. These are designed to address the need for a domestic representative spread product that isolates credit risk exposure while focusing on liquid securities, balancing capacity, tradeability and spread movement.

First, we create a reference basket of bank bonds, drawing the most liquid components from the FTSE Canada Corporate Financial Bank Bond index, part of the FTSE Canada Universe Bond index.

Second, we map the bonds in the reference basket to benchmark Government of Canada bonds.

Finally, we calculate the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index as the spread between the reference basket and the basket of parent bonds.

1 - Creation of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket

The selection process for the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket is highlighted in the infographic. We set the base universe, narrow it using a series of requirements designed to exclude less liquid or non-homogeneous issues, bucket the remaining securities by term and seniority and then weight them by market capitalisation.

Exhibit 2: Creation of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket

The reference basket is set at the end of February, May, August, and November and remains constant over each following quarter. If a security in the reference basket is subject to a default or a call by the issuer, it is removed on the last business day prior to the effective corporate action and its weight in the basket is reinvested proportionally into the remaining basket securities. If a security is downgraded to below investment grade, it remains in the basket until the next quarterly rebalancing date.

2 - Mapping to Government of Canada securities

At the quarterly index rebalancing date, each corporate bond in the reference basket (a “child”) is mapped to a benchmark Government of Canada bond (a “parent”). This mapping is based on the parent-child mapping scheme sourced from CanDeal Data and Analytics. The weights of the Government of Canada bonds are set as equal to the mapped corporate bonds, and the mapping is reported in the index files for transparency. A mapping validation is performed before each index rebalancing date to ensure that all bank bonds have a valid Government of Canada benchmark parent at that date.

Exhibit 3: Examples of parent-child mapping for FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index

3 - Calculation and aggregation of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index

Once the parent-child mapping is complete for the bonds in the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket, we calculate the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series as follows:

  • Spread value is calculated daily at 4 pm (using mid-market closing price) for each position in the basket;
  • Spread is calculated as yield-to-worst (YTW) on bank-issued corporate bond – YTW on Government of Canada bond;
  • Spread index is the market capitalisation-weighted average spread, based on the weights of the reference basket;
  • Spread index value serves as the basis for the listed futures contract on the index (see the section below, “Spread indices as the basis for futures contracts”).

Summary statistics for FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket

A back-test between June 2012 and March 2025 of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket methodology shows that the basket closely tracks the broader Canadian financials sector as measured by yield, spread, and duration. In recent history, the average coupon of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket has reflected the higher prevailing rates for newer bank issuances, given that the reference basket selects the most recently issued bond per issuer within each sector group (the sector groups are categorised by remaining term and seniority).

Exhibit 4: Summary statistics for FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket

Correlations of spread index to financials and corporates

A back-test of the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series methodology over the period from June 2012 to March 2025 also showed high correlations with the Canadian financials sector and with the corporate bond sector overall. This is unsurprising, given that Canadian bank bonds typically represent approximately 70% by weight of the financial sector (while financials, in turn, typically represent approximately 45% of the corporate bond market).

In other words, the implied spread embedded in the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index serves as a strong proxy for Canada’s corporate bond and financial bond sectors, with comparable levels of volatility.

Exhibit 5: Volatility and correlations of FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index

Please note:

  1. Spreads for Canada Corporate, Financial, Reference Basket, and US Bank are calculated using month-end index-level OAS measures.
  2. Spreads for Canada Bank Credit Spread Index are calculated as the weighted average YTW difference between bonds in the Reference Basket and their corresponding Government of Canada bonds.
  3. Spread volatility is calculated as the standard deviation of spread change.
  4. Implied spreads for Canada Corporate and Financial are calculated as the YTW difference measured against the Short-Term Federal Non-Agency Bond Index.
  5. For US Banks, daily volatility is inferred from monthly volatility, assuming 21 trading days per month

Spread indices as the basis for futures contracts

A new FTSE Canada Credit Spread index series will be created quarterly on the last business day of February, May, August, and November, based on the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Reference Basket. Each spread index series will expire four months after creation, in alignment with the life of the futures contract written on the series.

The futures contracts are expected to originate and settle between International Monetary Market (IMM) dates, which are the third Wednesday of each March, June, September, and December[4].

The FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series naming convention will include a three-character code indicating the delivery date. This will follow the standard ticker convention observed in the futures market, with the reference letters H, M, U, and Z corresponding to March, June, September, and December, respectively. For example:

  • FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index H25 (ticker: CBCH25) for an index series created on the last business day of November 2024 and maturing at the end of March 2025;
  • FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index M25 (ticker: CBCM25) for an index series created on the last business day of February 2025 and maturing at the end of June 2025;
  • FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index U25 (ticker: CBCU25) for an index series created on the last business day of May 2025 and maturing at the end of September 2025;
  • FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Z25 (ticker: CBCZ25) for an index series created on the last business day of August 2025 and maturing at the end of December 2025.

Exhibit 6: Sample timeline for reference basket rebalancing, index series, and related futures contracts

A risk transfer solution for the Canadian market

FTSE Russell is proud that the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index Series has been selected by the Montreal Exchange to serve as the foundation for a first-of-its-kind credit derivatives product, the Canada Bank Credit Index Futures (CBC), which will launch in 2026. These futures will provide market participants with a powerful tool to manage credit exposure, hedge risk and implement strategic investment views.

The futures are expected to serve a variety of uses:

  • Targeted credit exposure (as a way to gain direct exposure to the credit spread of Canadian banks)
  • Expanded yield curve access (by adding new, liquid points to the Canadian-listed yield curve, enhancing curve construction and trading precision)
  • Efficient hedging tool (to help manage spread risk, portfolio duration and liquidity needs)
  • Versatile trading strategies (to implement tactical credit views, replicate synthetic long/short credit positions or execute relative value trades)

Addressing Canadian client needs

FTSE Russell has created the FTSE Canada Bank Credit Spread Index to address the needs of Canadian financial market participants for a representative spread product that isolates domestic banks’ credit risk exposure while focusing on their most liquid fixed income securities. The index’s design balances capacity, tradeability and offer insights into credit risk dynamics within the Canadian investment grade market, facilitating informed decision-making for investors.

Footnotes

1 Credit spreads, economic activity and fragmentation.

2 See Timeline: The credit crunch of 2007/2008 | Reuters.

3 On average, we observed that financial bonds represent 44.7% of the all corporate sector of the FTSE Canada Universe Bond Index, where bank bonds represent 29.4% and 65.9% of the all corporate and financial sectors, respectively, based on daily data for the period 31 December 2003 through 31 May 2025. Source: FTSE Russell.

4 See FTSE Canada Bank Credit Futures (CBC) for more details.
